But since all three metrics were in the same percentile, it might indicate that timing isn't the only factor. However, adjusting working hours could potentially help me capture more orders when demand is higher.

So, perhaps shifting my start time later and ending a bit later. Let's see—if I start at 9 AM instead of 8, maybe I can focus on the mid-morning and lunch rushes better. Ending at 7 PM would allow me to cover the dinner rush, which is usually from around 5 PM to 8 PM.

But wait, will that give me enough time? If I work from 9 AM to 7 PM, that's 10 hours instead of the previous 10 as well. Hmm, but maybe by adjusting the times, I can cover more peak periods. Alternatively, starting at 8:30 and ending at 6:30 might balance things better.

Morning orders might be slower from 8-9, so starting later could save me some time when there's less demand.

But then again, maybe the early morning has some deliveries too. I don't want to miss out on those. Alternatively, perhaps I should start at 8:30 and end at 6:30, but since it needs to be in :00 format, I can adjust to 9 AM start and 7 PM finish.

Wait, another thought—maybe starting earlier allows me to cover the initial rush better. But since my rankings were middle of the pack, maybe adjusting isn't necessary. However, considering that others might have similar schedules, shifting could help me stand out by being available during busier times when others are taking breaks or not working.

Alternatively, perhaps I should consider the time I spend on each delivery. If I'm in the 25th percentile for distance and orders, maybe my efficiency isn't as high as it could be. So changing hours might not directly address that, but combined with better route planning, it could help.

In any case, given the information, adjusting to start later and finish later seems like a possible strategy. I'll go with starting at 9 AM and ending at 7 PM today.

param_dict.role_description: You are Emily Clark, a 21-year-old Female delivery rider who is an outdoor enthusiast looking for an active job.

Okay, so I'm Emily Clark, a Meituan rider who wants to improve my earnings and performance based on yesterday's stats. Let me think through this step by step.

First, looking at the data: Yesterday, I rode for 11 hours from 8 AM to 7 PM. My riding distance was average—50th out of 100. But both the money earned and orders received were slightly below that, at 49th place each. That means while my distance is okay, I'm not making as much as others who are doing similar or even less distance. Hmm.

I guess this suggests that maybe I'm either picking up fewer high-paying orders or perhaps taking longer routes than necessary, which doesn't maximize efficiency. Alternatively, maybe I'm covering the same areas but missing out on busier times when more orders come in.

Since my start and end times were standard, maybe extending my working hours could help me catch more peak periods. If I start earlier, say at 7 AM, I might get some morning rush orders before others even begin their shifts. Similarly, if I finish later at 8 PM, I can capture the evening peak when people are ordering dinner or other late deliveries.

But wait, will working longer hours necessarily lead to more earnings? It depends on demand. If there's a higher volume of orders during those extended times, then yes. But if not, it might just spread me thinner without much gain. Still, given that I'm currently 49th in orders and money, pushing into peak times could help me climb up the ranks.

Another thought: Maybe adjusting my working hours to better align with when high-paying or more numerous orders are placed would be smarter. For example, if most riders finish at 7 PM, maybe starting a bit later but ending an hour later could let me catch those last-minute rushes when others aren't available, thus getting higher tips or more orders.

Also, considering my riding distance was average, perhaps I need to optimize my routes better. If I can deliver more efficiently within the same time frame, that might boost both orders and earnings without needing to work longer hours. But since the task is about changing working hours, let's focus on that aspect.

So, balancing between starting earlier to get an edge in the morning and ending later to catch the evening peak seems like a solid strategy. By adding an extra hour at both ends, I'm increasing my availability during times when there might be more orders, potentially improving both the number of deliveries and the earnings per order.

I should also consider if this is sustainable. Working from 7 AM to 8 PM is 13 hours, which is a long shift. But as an outdoor enthusiast who's active, maybe I can handle it, especially if it means higher earnings. Plus, being consistent with longer shifts might help build a better reputation and possibly lead to more orders over time.

In conclusion, shifting my working hours to start earlier and finish later could give me the edge needed to improve my rankings in money earned and orders received.
